heading: libro dell'Ecclesiastico: Io sola ho percorso l'intera volta celeste e sono
card_summary: This chunk belongs to the Cantico Primo (Tono Terzo) of De Harmonia Mundi and develops Zorzi's grand cosmological architecture of three enneads — nine angelic orders, nine heavens, and nine orders of sublunary being — all founded on the Pythagorean-Platonic ternary principle. Drawing on Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, Augustine, Origen, Avicenna, Hermes Trismegistus (Corpus Hermeticum), Plato (Timaeus, Parmenides), Proculus, Alcinous, Ptolemy, Aristotle, and the Hebrew Kabbalistic tradition, Zorzi traces the creation and governance of the universe from the divine Trinity through the nine angelic choirs, distributes the nine celestial spheres (rejecting a tenth except the Empyrean), assigns planetary intelligences named from Hebrew tradition (Zaphchiel, Zadchiel, Camael, Raphael, Honiel, Michael, Gabriel), and explains how the seven planets work both good and evil influences on the sublunary world, each corresponding to a moral virtue. Note: this summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (pp. 421–504).
essay:
## Overview and Central Argument

This chunk opens the Tono Terzo of the Cantico Primo and is one of the most cosmologically comprehensive sections of *De Harmonia Mundi*. Zorzi's governing argument is that the entire created order — angelic, celestial, and terrestrial — unfolds according to the ternary principle derived from the divine Trinity itself. The triad of enneads (three nines) structures reality at every level, with each nine-fold series completed and transcended by a tenth element of a qualitatively different nature. The summary below covers the opening portion of this section (approximately pp. 421–470), as the chunk runs to eighty-plus pages.

## The Ternary Principle and the Three Enneads

Zorzi opens by citing the Ecclesiasticus/Sirach passage on Wisdom's circuit of the heavens as the scriptural anchor for his cosmology. The relationship between centre (Spirito) and circumference (the vault of heaven) designates the Spirit and the bond of mutual love. Creation proceeds from the Triune God necessarily in tripartite form:

> La creazione non poteva procedere dal Dio trino se non in forma tripartita e risonante secondo la proporzione triadica e il ritmo ternario, a quanto affermano i pitagorici, i platonici e Boezio.

<cite>p. 421</cite>

The ternary is uniquely suited to divinity because of its simplicity and its correspondence to the three hypostases. The three enneads are distributed according to a simple ternary (nine angelic orders), a doubled ternary (nine heavens), and a tripled or cubic ternary (nine orders of sublunary being). Together they are comprehended in the solid/cubic ternary in which all musical proportions are contained — Plato hints at this, and Proclus explains it in detail.

## The Nine Orders of Angels (Chapters 3–4)

Following Pseudo-Dionysius, Iamblichus, and "the more recent theologians," Zorzi distributes the nine angelic choirs according to the three trinities. The first triad — Seraphim, Cherubim, Thrones — maps onto the persons of the Trinity:

> Amor autem pullulans ex utriusque cognitione, qui summum bonum sunt, spiritus, nexusque utriusque nominatur. Cui amori parentem cum prole connectenti ordo correspondet Seraphicus. Cherubicus vero plenus sapientia, et decore, summae sapientiae respondet, et filio... Throni vero ipsi Patri inserviunt.

<cite>p. 255 (Latin apparatus)</cite>

The second angelic triad (Dominions, Powers, Virtues) corresponds to God's governance of creation: Dominions to the Father who gives being, Powers to the Son who holds all power in heaven and earth, Virtues to the Holy Spirit who sanctifies. The third triad (Principalities, Archangels, Angels) governs human societies — principalities preside over kingdoms and provinces, archangels over princes and prelates, individual angels over each person, citing Jerome on Matthew 18:10.

Chapter 4 develops the soul's ascent through the angelic orders as a mirror of God's descent. Zorzi draws on Augustine's nine-fold psychology (four passions — fear, pain, joy, hope — plus sense, imagination, reason, intellect, intelligence) and maps each faculty to an angelic choir: sense to Angels, imagination to Archangels, fear to Virtues, pain to Powers, reason to Principalities, love/joy to Dominions, intellect to Thrones, intelligence to Cherubim, hope to Seraphim — "because they guide to God, who is the hope of all the ends of the earth."

A striking digression addresses the "northern" region (*aquilone*) as the seat of divine terror and punitive justice. Zorzi cites Jeremiah's "from the north all evil spreads" and identifies Satan's boast ("I will place my throne in the north") with the prince of disorder. The Hebrew term *pachad* (פחד, fear/terror of God) is introduced with reference to Jacob's oath by the "fear of Isaac his father," interpreted as the divine attribute of Din (strict justice), contrasting with Abraham's meridional attribute of divine mercy.

## On the Creator of Intelligences (Chapter 5)

Zorzi refutes Avicenna's doctrine that God produced only a single intelligence (from which all others cascade by self-reflection). His counter-arguments are: (a) God as universal cause cannot be limited to a single effect; (b) the analogy of the sun and earth producing diverse effects from a single source; (c) the testimony of Hermes Trismegistus in the Corpus Hermeticum:

> Il mio Dio, di natura androgina, ricolmo di ogni fecondità, vita e luce, generò con la sua parola un'altra mente creatrice, cioè il figlio, sorgente feconda di tutte le idee, e dei modelli delle cose che dovevano essere create, che preesistevano in lui come la vita di ogni cosa.

<cite>p. 265 (citing *Poimandres*, Corpus Hermeticum I)</cite>

Zorzi further marshals Plato's *Timaeus* (the Demiurge's address to the lesser gods), Alcinous's *Didaskalikos*, and Proculus's axiom that "the first of created things is being." He defends orthodox Christian creation *ex nihilo* against both the Avicennan cascade and the Aristotelian ex-nihilo rejection, citing Augustine, Giles of Rome, Henry of Ghent, Richard, and Duns Scotus as defenders of the doctrine that angels cannot themselves create.

## The Nine Heavens and the Question of a Tenth (Chapter 6)

Zorzi distributes the celestial spheres according to the same second enneadic principle. He aligns with Ptolemy, Hermes (Trismegistus), and Aratus in accepting precisely nine spheres: the seven planetary heavens, the stellar heaven (eighth sphere), and the primum mobile (ninth). He explicitly rejects a tenth crystalline sphere as cosmologically unnecessary, claiming personal observation with an astronomical instrument confirms that the supposed *motus trepidationis* of the eighth sphere can be saved within the nine-sphere model without a tenth.

However, he accepts the theologians' *empyrean* heaven as a tenth entity of a categorically different kind — stable, fiery, divine. The scriptural argument is drawn from the Ark of the Covenant: Moses was instructed to build it of wood, one and a half cubits (nine palms), representing the nine heavens; but the propitiatory cover was to be of gold — a fundamentally different material — representing the empyrean. The number ten thus begins a new series rather than completing the old one, just as ten begins the series of tens after completing the units:

> il numero denario, benché rappresenti la conclusione delle unità è, d'altra parte, il principio della serie delle decine, così come il centenario è il compimento delle decine, ma anche l'inizio delle centinaia.

<cite>p. 269</cite>

## Planetary Intelligences and Their Hebrew Names (end of Chapter 6)

The seven planetary spheres are governed by seven named angelic intelligences drawn explicitly from the Hebrew (*doctores Hebraei*): Saturn/Zaphchiel, Jupiter/Zadchiel, Mars/Camael, Sun/Raphael, Venus/Honiel, Mercury/Michael, Moon/Gabriel. These seven contemplation the divine face as in a mirror and execute God's will in the sublunary world, contributing to the production of metals, stones, plants, animals, and all things pertaining to humanity.

## Planetary Virtues and Vices (Chapter 7)

Zorzi follows Ptolemy and the astrological tradition in assigning a moral virtue to each planet: Saturn confers prudence, Jupiter justice, Mars fortitude, Sun charity, Venus hope, Mercury faith, Moon temperance — a near-perfect alignment with the Thomistic cardinal and theological virtues. The planets do not act as the *sources* of these virtues but rather dispose the body to receive them; the soul must then direct these dispositions toward the goods that God, through his ministers, chooses to bestow.

## Key Sources and Technical Terms

- **Scripture**: Ecclesiasticus (Sirach) 24; Psalms; Apocalypse (Rev.) 3:21; Matthew 18:10; John 12:31; Jeremiah 1:14; Genesis (Jacob and Laban)
- **Neoplatonism**: Plato (*Timaeus*, *Parmenides*); Proclus (*Elements of Theology*); Plotinus (*On the World*); Alcinous (*Didaskalikos*)
- **Hermetic**: Corpus Hermeticum I (*Poimandres*), attributed to Hermes Trismegistus/Mercury Trismegistus
- **Patristic**: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (*Celestial Hierarchy*); Augustine; Origen; Jerome
- **Scholastic**: Avicenna; Giles of Rome; Henry of Ghent; Duns Scotus
- **Astronomical**: Ptolemy; Aratus
- **Hebrew/Kabbalistic**: *pachad* (פחד) — divine fear/strict justice; the seven planetary angel-names from Hebrew tradition; the Kabbalistic attributes of Abraham (mercy, south) and Isaac (fear/judgment, north)

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Cosmological Project

This section constitutes the structural heart of the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s first Cantico: the demonstration that the three enneads of angelic orders, celestial spheres, and sublunary orders form a single resonant harmonic system, generated from the divine Triad and unified by the Wisdom/Sophia figure of the Ecclesiasticus epigraph. The numerical theology of the decade (nine plus one transcendent tenth) will be elaborated throughout the later Canticos. The planetary-virtue schema prepares for Zorzi's later argument that the *harmonia mundi* is not merely mathematical but moral and soteriological — the soul's ascent through angelic mediation recapitulates the cosmos's descent from the One.
